name: Communication Studies
description: An elective course exploring the theory and practice of human communication across interpersonal, group, organizational, and mass media contexts.

modules.title: Communication Process & Models
modules.objectives: Identify the key elements of the communication process; compare and contrast linear, interactive, and transactional models of communication.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 1.2: The Communication Process
modules.practiceNote: Apply the transactional model by mapping a real conversation onto its elements
modules.assignment: Draw and label a transactional model of communication using a real conversation you had this week. Identify sender, receiver, message, channel, feedback, noise, and context. Write a 250-word reflection explaining how noise (physical or psychological) affected the exchange.

modules.title: Verbal Communication
modules.objectives: Explain how language creates meaning; analyze the impact of word choice, clarity, and language style on effective verbal communication.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 3.1: Language and Meaning
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Power of Words: Verbal Communication Skills
modules.practiceNote: Practice rewriting vague sentences using precise, inclusive, and clear language
modules.assignment: Collect five examples of ambiguous or misleading verbal messages from news headlines, advertisements, or social media. For each, rewrite the message to be clear and unambiguous, and explain in 50 words per example what made the original unclear.

modules.title: Nonverbal Communication
modules.objectives: Identify categories of nonverbal communication; analyze how body language, proxemics, and paralanguage convey meaning alongside verbal messages.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 4.1: Principles and Functions of Nonverbal Communication
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Your Body Language May Shape Who You Are (TED)
modules.practiceNote: Identify nonverbal cues in a silent video clip and explain their communicative function
modules.assignment: Watch a 5-minute segment of a TV show or film with the sound muted. Record at least 10 distinct nonverbal signals you observe (facial expressions, gestures, proxemics, touch, etc.) and write a 300-word analysis of what story the nonverbal communication tells without words.

modules.title: Listening & Feedback
modules.objectives: Distinguish between hearing and active listening; apply effective feedback strategies to improve communication outcomes.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 5.1: Understanding How and Why We Listen
modules.practiceNote: Practice the HURIER model steps using a recorded listening exercise
modules.assignment: Conduct a 10-minute conversation with a family member or friend on a topic they care about, using only active listening techniques (paraphrasing, open questions, minimal encouragers). Immediately after, write a 300-word reflection on what made active listening difficult, what you learned about the speaker, and how feedback shaped the conversation.

modules.title: Intrapersonal Communication
modules.objectives: Define intrapersonal communication and explain its role in self-concept, self-esteem, and internal dialogue.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 2.1: Self-Concept
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Power of Intrapersonal Communication
modules.practiceNote: Complete a self-concept inventory and reflect on how internal messages shape behavior
modules.assignment: Keep a 3-day intrapersonal journal: each day, record at least three instances of your internal self-talk (positive, negative, or neutral). At the end of the three days, write a 350-word analysis identifying patterns in your internal dialogue and how those messages influenced your communication with others.

modules.title: Interpersonal Communication
modules.objectives: Apply principles of interpersonal communication to build, maintain, and repair relationships; explain disclosure and reciprocity.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 6.1: Principles of Interpersonal Communication
modules.video2Note: YouTube — What Makes a Good Conversation (TED)
modules.practiceNote: Practice the Johari Window self-disclosure exercise with a partner
modules.assignment: Map your three closest relationships using the Johari Window model. For each relationship, identify what information sits in each quadrant (open, hidden, blind, unknown) and write a 300-word explanation of how varying levels of self-disclosure have shaped each relationship.

modules.title: Small Group Communication
modules.objectives: Identify stages of group development and member roles; analyze factors that contribute to or detract from group effectiveness.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 13.1: Understanding Small Groups
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Tuckman's Stages of Group Development
modules.practiceNote: Analyze a case study group using Tuckman's five stages model
modules.assignment: Think of a group project or team experience you have been part of (school, sport, club). Write a 400-word analysis applying Tuckman's stages (Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing, Adjourning) to that group, identifying at least two positive and two negative communication behaviors you observed.

modules.title: Organizational Communication
modules.objectives: Explain formal and informal communication flows within organizations; evaluate the impact of organizational culture on communication.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 14.1: Organizational Communication
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Organizational Communication: Formal and Informal Channels
modules.practiceNote: Create a communication flow diagram for a real or hypothetical organization
modules.assignment: Interview someone who works in an organization (parent, neighbor, community member) about how information flows in their workplace. Ask about formal channels (meetings, memos, email) and informal channels (grapevine, watercooler). Write a 350-word report comparing what they describe to the concepts of downward, upward, and horizontal communication.

modules.title: Intercultural Communication
modules.objectives: Identify cultural values and dimensions (Hofstede) that affect communication; develop strategies for communicating effectively across cultural differences.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 8.1: Foundations of Culture and Identity
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Hofstede's Cultural Dimensions Theory Explained
modules.practiceNote: Assess a cross-cultural misunderstanding scenario using Hofstede's dimensions
modules.assignment: Research two countries with contrasting scores on at least two of Hofstede's cultural dimensions (e.g., individualism vs. collectivism; high vs. low power distance). Write a 400-word essay explaining how those cultural differences could create communication challenges in a business or school setting, and propose two specific strategies to bridge those gaps.

modules.title: Mass Communication
modules.objectives: Define mass communication and distinguish it from interpersonal communication; explain key mass media theories such as agenda-setting and uses and gratifications.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 15.1: The Evolution of Media
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Agenda-Setting Theory in Mass Communication
modules.practiceNote: Apply uses and gratifications theory to your own social media consumption habits
modules.assignment: Track your media consumption for two days: record every media encounter (TV, streaming, social media, podcasts, newspapers) including duration and purpose. Write a 350-word analysis using uses and gratifications theory to explain why you consumed each type of media and what needs it fulfilled.

modules.title: Digital & Social Media
modules.objectives: Analyze how digital and social media platforms transform communication patterns; evaluate the effects of social media on identity and relationships.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 15.4: Social Media and Digital Communication
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The World Wide Web and Digital Communication
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Social Media is Changing Communication
modules.practiceNote: Analyze the comment section of a social media post for communication patterns
modules.assignment: Choose one social media platform you use regularly. Write a 400-word critical analysis examining: (1) how the platform's design shapes communication behavior, (2) one positive and one negative effect it has on interpersonal relationships, and (3) how your online self-presentation differs from your face-to-face self-presentation.

modules.title: Persuasion
modules.objectives: Identify the elements of persuasive communication (ethos, pathos, logos); apply persuasion theories including the Elaboration Likelihood Model.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 11.1: Persuasion — An Overview
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Ethos, Pathos, and Logos
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Science of Persuasion (Cialdini's Six Principles)
modules.practiceNote: Analyze a persuasive advertisement identifying ethos, pathos, and logos appeals
modules.assignment: Find three advertisements (TV, print, or online) and analyze each one using Aristotle's rhetorical appeals. Write a 350-word essay identifying which appeal dominates each ad, why the advertiser chose that approach for their audience, and which ad you find most persuasive and why.

modules.title: Conflict & Negotiation
modules.objectives: Identify styles of conflict management; apply negotiation strategies to resolve communication conflicts constructively.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 6.4: Conflict and Interpersonal Communication
modules.practiceNote: Role-play a conflict scenario using principled negotiation techniques
modules.assignment: Recall a real conflict you experienced or witnessed. Write a 400-word case study: describe the conflict, identify which Thomas-Kilmann style (competing, collaborating, compromising, avoiding, accommodating) each party used, evaluate whether the outcome was effective, and describe how a collaborating approach might have produced a better result.

modules.title: Communication Ethics
modules.objectives: Apply ethical principles to communication choices; evaluate real-world cases involving honesty, privacy, media responsibility, and digital conduct.
modules.readingNote: Communication in the Real World (UMN OER) — Ch 1.4: Communication Ethics
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Ethics and Responsibility in Digital Communication
modules.practiceNote: Evaluate three ethical dilemmas in communication using the NCA Credo for Ethical Communication
modules.assignment: Find a recent news story involving an ethical communication dilemma (e.g., a public figure's misleading statement, a privacy breach, cyberbullying, deepfakes). Write a 400-word ethical analysis using at least two ethical principles from the NCA Credo for Ethical Communication, discuss the harm caused, and propose what ethical communication would have looked like in that situation.

quizQuestions.question: Which model of communication depicts communication as a two-way process with feedback?
options: Linear model
Interactive model
Transactional model
Shannon-Weaver model
quizQuestions.explanation: The interactive model adds feedback to the linear model, making communication a two-way exchange rather than a one-way transmission.

quizQuestions.question: In the communication process, 'noise' refers to:
options: Only loud sounds that interrupt speech
Any interference that distorts the message
The tone of voice used by the sender
The medium through which a message travels
quizQuestions.explanation: Noise is any physical, psychological, or semantic interference that distorts or disrupts the accurate transmission and reception of a message.

quizQuestions.question: The transactional model of communication emphasizes that:
options: Communication flows only from sender to receiver
Both parties send and receive messages simultaneously
Feedback is optional in real conversations
Context has no influence on message interpretation
quizQuestions.explanation: The transactional model treats communication as a simultaneous, mutually influential process where both parties are senders and receivers at the same time.

quizQuestions.question: Which element of the communication process refers to the route through which a message travels?
options: Sender
Feedback
Channel
Encoding
quizQuestions.explanation: The channel is the medium (e.g., face-to-face, telephone, email, radio) through which a message is transmitted from sender to receiver.

quizQuestions.question: The original linear model of communication was developed by:
options: Harold Lasswell and Shannon & Weaver
Aristotle and Plato
Marshall McLuhan and Noam Chomsky
George Gerbner and Paul Lazarsfeld
quizQuestions.explanation: Harold Lasswell's model (who says what, to whom, through which channel, with what effect) and Shannon & Weaver's mathematical model are both foundational linear models.

quizQuestions.question: The study of the relationship between language and meaning is called:
options: Syntax
Phonology
Semantics
Pragmatics
quizQuestions.explanation: Semantics is the branch of linguistics concerned with meaning — how words and sentences carry and convey meaning.

quizQuestions.question: Which type of language refers to the emotional associations a word carries beyond its dictionary definition?
options: Denotative meaning
Connotative meaning
Abstract language
Equivocal language
quizQuestions.explanation: Connotative meaning includes the emotional, cultural, or personal associations attached to a word, beyond its literal denotative definition.

quizQuestions.question: Using vague or abstract words that can be interpreted in multiple ways is called using:
options: Euphemistic language
Equivocal language
Concrete language
Jargon
quizQuestions.explanation: Equivocal language consists of words or phrases that have more than one commonly accepted meaning, which can cause misunderstanding.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of inclusive language?
options: Using 'mankind' to refer to all people
Using 'they' as a singular gender-neutral pronoun
Addressing a mixed group as 'you guys'
Using job titles like 'stewardess' instead of 'flight attendant'
quizQuestions.explanation: Using 'they' as a singular gender-neutral pronoun is a form of inclusive language that avoids assuming gender and includes all people.

quizQuestions.question: Euphemistic language is best described as:
options: Language that exaggerates meaning for effect
Technical vocabulary used within a specialized field
Mild or indirect expressions substituted for blunt ones
Language that repeats the same idea in different words
quizQuestions.explanation: Euphemisms are mild or indirect expressions (e.g., 'passed away' for 'died') used to make uncomfortable topics more palatable.

quizQuestions.question: Albert Mehrabian's research suggested that in face-to-face communication, the majority of emotional meaning is conveyed through:
options: The words spoken
Tone of voice and body language
Written follow-up messages
The channel selected
quizQuestions.explanation: Mehrabian's oft-cited (though frequently overgeneralized) research suggested that tone of voice and body language carry the bulk of emotional meaning in face-to-face interactions.

quizQuestions.question: The study of personal space and how people use physical distance in communication is called:
options: Kinesics
Haptics
Proxemics
Chronemics
quizQuestions.explanation: Proxemics, developed by Edward Hall, is the study of how people perceive and use space in communication, including intimate, personal, social, and public zones.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of paralanguage?
options: A gesture that replaces a spoken word
The rate, pitch, and volume of speech
The distance maintained between speakers
Eye contact patterns
quizQuestions.explanation: Paralanguage refers to vocal elements that accompany spoken words, including rate, pitch, volume, and intonation, but not the words themselves.

quizQuestions.question: A gesture that completely replaces a word or phrase (e.g., a thumbs up) is called an:
options: Illustrator
Emblem
Adaptor
Regulator
quizQuestions.explanation: Emblems are gestures with direct verbal translations that are well understood within a cultural group and can stand alone in place of spoken words.

quizQuestions.question: Nonverbal communication that manages the flow of conversation (e.g., nodding to indicate 'keep talking') is called:
options: Affect displays
Regulators
Adaptors
Emblems
quizQuestions.explanation: Regulators are nonverbal cues that control the flow of communication, signaling turn-taking, agreement, and the desire to speak or stop speaking.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes active listening?
options: Hearing the words spoken while multitasking
Fully concentrating on, understanding, and responding to a speaker
Waiting silently until the speaker finishes to plan your reply
Agreeing with everything the speaker says
quizQuestions.explanation: Active listening involves full attention, understanding, and engaged response to the speaker, going beyond mere hearing to genuine comprehension.

quizQuestions.question: Paraphrasing in active listening means:
options: Repeating the speaker's exact words back to them
Restating the speaker's message in your own words to confirm understanding
Asking a new question to change the topic
Summarizing only the emotional content of the message
quizQuestions.explanation: Paraphrasing involves restating the speaker's message in your own words to confirm you understood correctly and show you were paying attention.

quizQuestions.question: Which listening barrier involves mentally preparing your response while the other person is still speaking?
options: Selective listening
Pseudo-listening
Rehearsal
Information overload
quizQuestions.explanation: Rehearsal is the barrier where the listener is mentally composing a reply rather than fully attending to what the speaker is saying.

quizQuestions.question: Evaluative feedback in communication is best described as feedback that:
options: Describes behavior without judgment
Judges or assesses the worth of the message or the speaker
Asks for clarification about the message
Reflects the speaker's emotions back to them
quizQuestions.explanation: Evaluative feedback makes a judgment about the message or person (e.g., 'That was a bad idea'), as opposed to descriptive or empathic feedback.

quizQuestions.question: The acronym HURIER stands for:
options: Hearing, Understanding, Remembering, Interpreting, Evaluating, Responding
Helping, Using, Reading, Interpreting, Engaging, Reflecting
Hearing, Using, Responding, Interpreting, Evaluating, Reviewing
Helping, Understanding, Reading, Integrating, Elaborating, Rewording
quizQuestions.explanation: HURIER is a six-step model of listening: Hearing, Understanding, Remembering, Interpreting, Evaluating, and Responding.

quizQuestions.question: Intrapersonal communication refers to:
options: Communication between two close friends
Communication that occurs within one's own mind
Communication in large public settings
Communication through mass media
quizQuestions.explanation: Intrapersonal communication is the internal dialogue and self-talk that occurs within an individual's own mind.

quizQuestions.question: Self-concept is best defined as:
options: How others perceive you
Your overall subjective evaluation of your own worth
The mental image you hold of yourself
Your awareness of others' feelings
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-concept is the mental image or perception an individual holds of themselves, encompassing beliefs about one's personality, abilities, and roles.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of negative self-talk?
options: 'I can improve with more practice.'
'I always mess things up.'
'This is challenging but I can handle it.'
'I made a mistake but I will learn from it.'
quizQuestions.explanation: Negative self-talk involves critical, defeatist internal messages (e.g., 'I always mess things up') that can damage self-esteem and communication confidence.

quizQuestions.question: The 'looking-glass self' concept, developed by Charles Cooley, suggests that our self-concept is shaped by:
options: Our genetic predispositions
How we imagine others perceive us
Our earliest childhood memories
Our comparison to idealized media images
quizQuestions.explanation: The looking-glass self theory holds that individuals form their self-concept based on how they believe others perceive and evaluate them.

quizQuestions.question: Self-esteem is best distinguished from self-concept as:
options: The roles you play in different contexts
An evaluative judgment about your own worth or value
A neutral description of your characteristics
The image you present to others
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-esteem is the evaluative component of self-perception — how positively or negatively you judge your own worth, separate from the neutral descriptive self-concept.

quizQuestions.question: The Johari Window model helps individuals understand:
options: How messages are encoded and decoded
The relationship between self-disclosure and mutual awareness
The stages of group development
How conflict escalates in organizations
quizQuestions.explanation: The Johari Window is a tool for understanding self-disclosure and interpersonal awareness, depicting information known/unknown to self and others in four quadrants.

quizQuestions.question: Self-disclosure in interpersonal communication refers to:
options: Revealing information about yourself to others
Listening carefully to what another person says
Keeping personal information private in professional settings
Offering feedback on another person's behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-disclosure is the intentional act of sharing personal information — thoughts, feelings, or experiences — with another person.

quizQuestions.question: Social Penetration Theory, developed by Altman and Taylor, describes relationships as progressing through:
options: Five fixed stages that always occur in order
Increasing breadth and depth of self-disclosure over time
Cycles of conflict and resolution
Networks of overlapping social roles
quizQuestions.explanation: Social Penetration Theory uses an 'onion' metaphor, proposing that relationships develop as self-disclosure increases in both breadth (topics) and depth (intimacy).

quizQuestions.question: In interpersonal communication, reciprocity refers to:
options: Responding to disclosure with similar levels of disclosure
Avoiding self-disclosure in professional relationships
Repeating a message to ensure it is understood
Mimicking the nonverbal behavior of your conversation partner
quizQuestions.explanation: Reciprocity is the social norm in which people tend to match the level of self-disclosure offered by their conversation partner, building mutual trust.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes a relationship at the 'stable exchange' stage of Social Penetration Theory?
options: Two people just met and share only surface-level information
Two people freely share deep personal information across many topics
Two people are ending their relationship and withdrawing disclosures
Two people are in formal conflict and refusing to communicate
quizQuestions.explanation: The stable exchange stage features partners who have developed trust and routinely share deep, wide-ranging personal information across numerous topics.

quizQuestions.question: According to Tuckman's model, in which stage do group members first experience conflict and power struggles?
options: Forming
Storming
Norming
Performing
quizQuestions.explanation: In the Storming stage, group members begin to assert ideas and compete for positions, leading to interpersonal conflict and tension before norms are established.

quizQuestions.question: A 'task role' in small group communication is best described as a role that:
options: Maintains emotional harmony within the group
Helps the group achieve its goals and complete work
Promotes individual interests over group goals
Provides social support to discouraged members
quizQuestions.explanation: Task roles (e.g., initiator, information seeker, clarifier) are behaviors that help the group accomplish its assigned work and reach its objectives.

quizQuestions.question: Groupthink refers to:
options: Effective collaboration resulting from good group norms
The tendency of cohesive groups to prioritize harmony over critical analysis
The process of brainstorming to generate many ideas
Assigning roles to members based on their strengths
quizQuestions.explanation: Groupthink occurs when the desire for conformity and group harmony causes members to suppress dissent and avoid critical evaluation of decisions.

quizQuestions.question: The ideal number of members for effective small group communication is generally considered to be:
options: 2–3 members
3–9 members
10–20 members
Over 20 members
quizQuestions.explanation: Communication scholars generally consider 3–9 members optimal for small groups, allowing diverse perspectives while maintaining manageable interaction.

quizQuestions.question: In Tuckman's model, the Norming stage is characterized by:
options: Initial introductions and polite interaction
Conflict and competition for roles
Establishment of rules and group cohesion
High productivity and effective problem-solving
quizQuestions.explanation: During Norming, group members resolve earlier conflicts, establish shared norms and expectations, and develop a sense of group cohesion and identity.

quizQuestions.question: Downward communication in an organization flows:
options: From employees to management
From managers to subordinates
Between departments at the same level
From the organization to external stakeholders
quizQuestions.explanation: Downward communication flows from higher levels of the organizational hierarchy (managers) to lower levels (subordinates), conveying instructions and policies.

quizQuestions.question: The 'grapevine' in organizational communication refers to:
options: Official memos circulated through management channels
Informal information networks among employees
A visual chart of the organization's structure
A formal process for filing employee grievances
quizQuestions.explanation: The organizational grapevine is the informal, unofficial network of communication among employees that travels outside of formal channels.

quizQuestions.question: Horizontal communication in an organization refers to communication:
options: From executives to all employees
Between people at the same hierarchical level
From employees to top management
Between the organization and the public
quizQuestions.explanation: Horizontal (or lateral) communication occurs between employees, departments, or teams at the same level of the organizational hierarchy.

quizQuestions.question: Organizational culture is best defined as:
options: The formal rules and regulations of an organization
The shared values, beliefs, and practices that shape behavior in an organization
The official communication channels of an organization
The demographic makeup of an organization's workforce
quizQuestions.explanation: Organizational culture refers to the shared values, assumptions, beliefs, and norms that guide behavior and communication within an organization.

quizQuestions.question: Information overload in organizations occurs when:
options: Employees receive no communication from management
The volume of information exceeds individuals' ability to process it effectively
Communication flows only downward without feedback
Employees rely too heavily on informal communication networks
quizQuestions.explanation: Information overload happens when the amount of information received surpasses a person's capacity to process it, leading to reduced decision quality and stress.

quizQuestions.question: Geert Hofstede's cultural dimension that describes the extent to which individuals versus groups are prioritized is called:
options: Power distance
Uncertainty avoidance
Individualism vs. collectivism
Masculinity vs. femininity
quizQuestions.explanation: Hofstede's individualism vs. collectivism dimension measures whether a culture prioritizes individual goals (individualism) or group harmony and loyalty (collectivism).

quizQuestions.question: In high-context cultures, communication relies heavily on:
options: Explicit, direct verbal messages
Implicit context, relationships, and nonverbal cues
Written formal contracts and documents
Technical precision and exact word choice
quizQuestions.explanation: High-context cultures (common in East Asia, Middle East) rely on shared understanding, nonverbal cues, and context rather than explicit verbal messages.

quizQuestions.question: Ethnocentrism in intercultural communication refers to:
options: Appreciation and celebration of cultural differences
The belief that one's own culture is superior to others
The ability to adapt communication style across cultures
The objective study of different cultural practices
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethnocentrism is the tendency to judge other cultures by the standards of one's own culture and to consider one's own culture superior.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes cultural relativism?
options: Judging other cultures by the standards of one's own
Evaluating cultural practices within their own cultural context
Adopting the behaviors of a host culture
Combining elements of two cultures into a new hybrid culture
quizQuestions.explanation: Cultural relativism is the practice of understanding and evaluating cultural beliefs and practices within their own cultural context, without outside judgment.

quizQuestions.question: Hofstede's power distance dimension measures:
options: The degree to which less powerful members accept unequal power distribution
The degree to which cultures avoid uncertainty
Whether a culture is more masculine or feminine in orientation
How short-term or long-term oriented a culture is
quizQuestions.explanation: Power distance measures the extent to which less powerful members of a society accept and expect that power is distributed unequally.

quizQuestions.question: Agenda-setting theory proposes that mass media:
options: Directly tells audiences what to think about issues
Determines which issues audiences consider important by the emphasis given to them
Changes audience political opinions through repeated exposure
Provides entertainment that has no influence on real-world beliefs
quizQuestions.explanation: Agenda-setting theory (McCombs & Shaw) proposes that media influences the salience of topics — not what people think, but what they think about.

quizQuestions.question: Uses and Gratifications Theory focuses on:
options: How media organizations decide what news to report
Why audiences actively choose media to satisfy specific needs
The effects of violent media content on behavior
The economic structure of media industries
quizQuestions.explanation: Uses and Gratifications Theory examines why and how individuals actively choose and use media to fulfill specific psychological and social needs.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best distinguishes mass communication from interpersonal communication?
options: Mass communication uses verbal messages; interpersonal does not
Mass communication reaches large, heterogeneous audiences through technology
Interpersonal communication requires written language
Mass communication always involves face-to-face interaction
quizQuestions.explanation: Mass communication involves transmitting messages to large, diverse audiences through media technologies, unlike interpersonal communication which is direct and personal.

quizQuestions.question: Gatekeeping in mass communication refers to:
options: The process of encrypting media content for security
The process by which editors and journalists decide which stories get published
Government regulation of broadcast licenses
Audience selection of preferred media channels
quizQuestions.explanation: Gatekeeping is the process by which media professionals (editors, producers) select, filter, and decide which information reaches the audience.

quizQuestions.question: The term 'mass media' refers to:
options: Communication between two individuals using technology
Communication channels that reach large audiences simultaneously
Personal diaries and journals distributed privately
Face-to-face communication in large public gatherings
quizQuestions.explanation: Mass media are communication channels (TV, radio, newspapers, internet) designed to reach and deliver messages to large, diverse audiences simultaneously.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of 'parasocial relationships' describes:
options: Mutual friendships formed through social media interaction
One-sided emotional connections formed with media figures
Professional relationships conducted entirely online
Relationships between social media influencers and their sponsors
quizQuestions.explanation: Parasocial relationships are one-sided connections that audience members develop with media figures (celebrities, TV characters) who are unaware of their existence.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'digital divide'?
options: The difference between traditional and digital journalism
The gap between those with and without access to digital technology
The separation of personal and professional social media accounts
The difference in content quality between TV and online media
quizQuestions.explanation: The digital divide refers to the gap between individuals and communities that have access to digital technology (internet, devices) and those that do not.

quizQuestions.question: Echo chambers in social media refer to environments where:
options: Users are exposed to a wide variety of opposing viewpoints
Users are primarily exposed to information that confirms existing beliefs
Algorithms promote controversial and polarizing content
Social networks connect strangers with widely different backgrounds
quizQuestions.explanation: Echo chambers are online spaces where algorithms and user choices filter content so that individuals primarily encounter opinions and information aligned with their existing views.

quizQuestions.question: Asynchronous digital communication is best described as communication that:
options: Occurs in real time with immediate responses
Does not require simultaneous participation from all parties
Requires video and audio technology to function
Is limited to 280 characters per message
quizQuestions.explanation: Asynchronous communication (e.g., email, recorded video) does not require participants to be present at the same time, allowing for delayed responses.

quizQuestions.question: The term 'digital footprint' refers to:
options: The physical environmental impact of electronic devices
The trail of data left by a person's online activities
The number of followers on a social media account
A measure of how frequently a user posts online
quizQuestions.explanation: A digital footprint is the record of data — posts, searches, clicks — that an individual leaves behind through their online activities.

quizQuestions.question: Aristotle's three modes of persuasion are:
options: Logos, Ethos, Pathos
Logic, Emotion, Authority
Facts, Values, Policies
Clarity, Brevity, Accuracy
quizQuestions.explanation: Aristotle identified three rhetorical appeals: Logos (logic and evidence), Ethos (credibility and character), and Pathos (emotional appeal).

quizQuestions.question: Ethos in persuasive communication refers to the speaker's:
options: Emotional connection with the audience
Logical reasoning and use of evidence
Credibility, trustworthiness, and expertise
Understanding of the audience's cultural background
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethos is the appeal to credibility — the audience's perception of the speaker's expertise, character, and trustworthiness.

quizQuestions.question: The Elaboration Likelihood Model (ELM) proposes two routes to persuasion:
options: Emotional and rational routes
Central and peripheral routes
Conscious and subconscious routes
Verbal and nonverbal routes
quizQuestions.explanation: The ELM (Petty & Cacioppo) identifies the central route (careful, effortful processing of arguments) and the peripheral route (quick judgment based on superficial cues).

quizQuestions.question: Robert Cialdini's principle of 'social proof' suggests that people are persuaded by:
options: Authoritative statistics from credible scientific sources
Evidence that many other people are doing or believing something
Logical arguments that demonstrate long-term benefit
The physical attractiveness of the communicator
quizQuestions.explanation: Social proof is the tendency to look to the behavior of others as evidence of the correct action, especially in uncertain situations (e.g., '9 out of 10 dentists recommend...').

quizQuestions.question: A 'two-sided message' in persuasion is effective because it:
options: Avoids mentioning any weaknesses of the speaker's position
Presents the opposing argument and then refutes it, boosting credibility
Uses only emotional appeals without logical evidence
Relies on the authority of an expert to deliver the message
quizQuestions.explanation: Two-sided messages acknowledge counterarguments and then refute them, which increases the speaker's perceived credibility and makes the message more persuasive for informed audiences.

quizQuestions.question: According to the Thomas-Kilmann model, which conflict style is high in both assertiveness and cooperativeness?
options: Competing
Avoiding
Compromising
Collaborating
quizQuestions.explanation: The collaborating style seeks a win-win solution by being both fully assertive about one's own needs and fully cooperative in addressing the other party's needs.

quizQuestions.question: The avoiding conflict style is characterized by:
options: High concern for self and low concern for others
Low assertiveness and low cooperativeness
Equal sharing of sacrifice by both parties
High focus on meeting the other party's needs
quizQuestions.explanation: The avoiding style involves withdrawing from conflict, neither asserting one's own needs nor cooperating with the other party, effectively postponing or sidestepping the issue.

quizQuestions.question: Principled negotiation, as described in 'Getting to Yes,' focuses on:
options: Arguing firmly for your stated position until the other side yields
Separating people from problems and focusing on interests not positions
Finding the middle point between two extreme demands
Avoiding negotiation and seeking a third-party decision
quizQuestions.explanation: Principled negotiation (Fisher & Ury) focuses on separating people from problems, addressing underlying interests rather than stated positions, and creating mutual gains.

quizQuestions.question: When conflict escalates because each party's hostile response provokes an even more hostile response, this is called:
options: Stonewalling
Reciprocal escalation
Avoidance cycle
Pseudo-conflict
quizQuestions.explanation: Reciprocal escalation (or escalation spiral) occurs when each party responds to the other's negativity with greater negativity, intensifying the conflict over time.

quizQuestions.question: Pseudo-conflict arises when:
options: Two parties have deeply incompatible values and goals
Two parties perceive a conflict that does not actually exist due to misunderstanding
Conflict is deliberately provoked for competitive advantage
A mediator incorrectly identifies the source of a dispute
quizQuestions.explanation: Pseudo-conflict is apparent rather than real conflict, arising from misunderstanding or misperception rather than genuine incompatibility of goals or values.

quizQuestions.question: The NCA Credo for Ethical Communication emphasizes which of the following core principles?
options: Winning arguments at any cost
Truthfulness, accuracy, honesty, and reasonableness in communication
Protecting organizational reputation above all else
Using persuasion to achieve favorable outcomes
quizQuestions.explanation: The NCA Credo for Ethical Communication holds that ethical communicators are truthful, accurate, honest, and reasonable, and that they promote access to information and free expression.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of an ethical violation in digital communication?
options: Paraphrasing a source and citing it properly
Sharing someone's private messages without their consent
Using data from a public government website in a report
Posting a correction when you shared inaccurate information
quizQuestions.explanation: Sharing private messages without consent violates privacy rights and ethical communication principles of respect for persons and confidentiality.

quizQuestions.question: Media responsibility refers to the obligation of mass media to:
options: Maximize profit through advertising revenue
Operate honestly, fairly, and in the public interest
Report only positive news to maintain audience morale
Prioritize entertainment over information
quizQuestions.explanation: Media responsibility encompasses the ethical obligations of media organizations to provide accurate, fair, balanced information and to act in the public interest.

quizQuestions.question: Plagiarism in communication is defined as:
options: Quoting a source directly with proper citation
Presenting another person's ideas or words as your own without attribution
Summarizing an argument in simpler language
Using widely known facts that require no citation
quizQuestions.explanation: Plagiarism is the unethical practice of using another person's words, ideas, or creative work without proper attribution, presenting it as one's own.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best illustrates ethical intercultural communication?
options: Assuming your culture's communication norms are universally appropriate
Adapting communication style to respect cultural norms while maintaining honesty
Avoiding interaction with people from different cultural backgrounds
Translating messages literally without regard for cultural context
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethical intercultural communication involves adapting one's style to respect cultural differences while remaining truthful and treating all parties with dignity.

questions.question: Which communication model is most realistic because it shows both parties simultaneously sending and receiving messages?
options: Linear model
Interactive model
Transactional model
Aristotle's model
questions.explanation: The transactional model reflects the simultaneous, mutually influential nature of real communication, where all parties act as senders and receivers at the same time.

questions.question: Semantic noise is best described as:
options: Loud environmental sounds that interrupt speech
Differences in word meaning that cause misunderstanding
Technical problems with communication technology
Physical distractions in the communication environment
questions.explanation: Semantic noise occurs when words or symbols mean different things to the sender and receiver, causing the message to be misinterpreted.

questions.question: The difference between denotative and connotative meaning is that:
options: Denotative meaning is emotional; connotative meaning is literal
Denotative meaning is the dictionary definition; connotative meaning includes emotional associations
Connotative meaning is precise; denotative meaning is vague
They refer to the same concept from different communication traditions
questions.explanation: Denotative meaning is the objective, dictionary definition of a word; connotative meaning includes the subjective, emotional, and cultural associations the word carries.

questions.question: Proxemics is the study of how people use:
options: Facial expressions to convey emotion
Physical space and distance in communication
Vocal tone and rate in conversation
Eye contact to regulate conversation
questions.explanation: Proxemics, developed by Edward T. Hall, is the study of how individuals perceive and use physical space in their social interactions.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of active listening?
options: Maintaining eye contact with the speaker
Paraphrasing to confirm understanding
Internally planning your response while the speaker talks
Using minimal encouragers like nodding
questions.explanation: Planning your response while someone is talking (rehearsal) is a barrier to active listening, not a characteristic of it; active listeners focus fully on the speaker.

questions.question: The 'looking-glass self' theory suggests that self-concept develops through:
options: Comparison with idealized images in media
Imagining how others perceive and evaluate us
Biological and genetic predispositions
Childhood experiences with parents and authority figures
questions.explanation: Charles Cooley's looking-glass self theory proposes that individuals form self-concept based on how they imagine they appear to others and how they imagine others judge them.

questions.question: The Johari Window quadrant labeled 'Hidden Area' contains information that:
options: Is known to both self and others
Is known to self but not shared with others
Is unknown to self but known to others
Is unknown to both self and others
questions.explanation: The Hidden Area of the Johari Window contains information the person knows about themselves but has not disclosed to others, representing guarded or private information.

questions.question: Social Penetration Theory uses an onion metaphor to illustrate that relationships deepen through:
options: Conflict and resolution cycles
Increasing breadth and depth of self-disclosure
Physical proximity and repeated contact
Shared group membership and roles
questions.explanation: Social Penetration Theory (Altman & Taylor) describes relationship development as peeling back layers of an onion as self-disclosure increases in breadth and depth over time.

questions.question: In which stage of Tuckman's group development model does the group reach its highest level of productivity?
options: Forming
Storming
Norming
Performing
questions.explanation: The Performing stage is when the group has resolved interpersonal issues, established roles, and is functioning at peak effectiveness toward its goals.

questions.question: Groupthink is most likely to occur in groups that are:
options: Large, diverse, and newly formed
Highly cohesive and under pressure to reach quick consensus
Made up of members with low investment in outcomes
Led by a leader who actively encourages dissent
questions.explanation: Groupthink is most common in highly cohesive groups facing pressure, where maintaining harmony becomes more important than evaluating alternatives critically.

questions.question: Horizontal communication within an organization flows between:
options: Executives and board members
Departments or individuals at the same hierarchical level
Managers and their direct reports
The organization and external stakeholders
questions.explanation: Horizontal or lateral communication moves between peers — departments or employees at the same level — coordinating efforts across the organization.

questions.question: Which type of communication behavior would be classified as an 'informal channel' in organizational communication?
options: A company-wide email from the CEO
An employee performance review meeting
Colleagues gossiping in the break room
A formal policy announcement on the company intranet
questions.explanation: The grapevine (gossip, informal conversations) is an informal communication channel that exists outside official organizational structures.

questions.question: In high-context cultures, communication tends to rely primarily on:
options: Explicit verbal statements and written contracts
Implicit understanding, context, and nonverbal signals
Precise technical language and formal vocabulary
Direct confrontation and clear expression of disagreement
questions.explanation: High-context cultures depend on shared context, relationships, and nonverbal cues to convey meaning, with less reliance on explicit verbal messages.

questions.question: Hofstede's individualism dimension is characterized by cultures that emphasize:
options: Group loyalty, collective harmony, and shared identity
Personal achievement, self-reliance, and individual rights
Respect for elders and traditional authority structures
Long-term planning and future-oriented values
questions.explanation: Individualist cultures (e.g., US, Australia) prioritize personal achievement, autonomy, and individual rights over group loyalty and collective goals.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an emblem in nonverbal communication?
options: Scratching your nose when nervous
Using hand gestures to emphasize a spoken point
Giving a thumbs-up to mean 'OK' or 'good'
Adjusting your posture when the topic becomes serious
questions.explanation: Emblems are gestures with specific, culturally shared verbal translations that can stand alone without spoken words, such as the thumbs-up for approval.

questions.question: The Shannon-Weaver model of communication was originally designed to model:
options: Human face-to-face conversation
Telephone and electronic signal transmission
Mass media broadcast communication
Organizational information flow
questions.explanation: Shannon and Weaver's mathematical model (1949) was developed to solve problems in telephone and electronic transmission, then adopted by communication scholars.

questions.question: Abstract language is problematic in communication because it:
options: Is too technical for most audiences to understand
Lacks specificity and can be interpreted in multiple ways
Carries strong negative connotations for listeners
Relies too heavily on nonverbal cues for meaning
questions.explanation: Abstract language (e.g., 'improve performance' vs. 'submit reports by 5 PM') lacks the specific referents needed for a shared, unambiguous meaning.

questions.question: Kinesics is the study of:
options: The use of physical space in communication
Body movement and gesture as communication
The meaning of touch in social interaction
Time as a communicative resource
questions.explanation: Kinesics is the study of body language — gestures, facial expressions, eye contact, and posture — as forms of nonverbal communication.

questions.question: Which listening style involves focusing exclusively on facts and data while filtering out emotional content?
options: Empathic listening
Appreciative listening
Comprehensive listening
Analytical listening
questions.explanation: Analytical (or critical) listening focuses on evaluating and analyzing the informational content of a message, often setting aside emotional dimensions.

questions.question: Cultivation Theory, developed by George Gerbner, proposes that:
options: Heavy TV viewers come to perceive the world as reflecting TV's version of reality
People choose media that confirms their existing political beliefs
Media audiences are passive recipients of media messages
Social media cultivates new forms of democratic participation
questions.explanation: Cultivation Theory proposes that heavy, long-term television viewing shapes viewers' perceptions of social reality to align more closely with the world depicted on TV.

questions.question: Agenda-setting theory differs from cultivation theory in that agenda-setting focuses on:
options: Long-term attitudinal effects of media violence
Which issues audiences think are important rather than worldview formation
The economic motivations of media corporations
Direct behavioral changes caused by media exposure
questions.explanation: Agenda-setting focuses on issue salience — what topics audiences prioritize — while cultivation theory addresses the long-term impact of media on worldview.

questions.question: Filter bubbles in digital communication refer to:
options: Privacy settings that hide personal data from advertisers
Algorithm-driven information environments where users see only content aligned with their views
Content moderation systems that remove harmful material
Tools that allow users to block unwanted communications
questions.explanation: Filter bubbles are the result of personalization algorithms that curate content matching users' preferences, limiting exposure to diverse perspectives.

questions.question: Which of Cialdini's six principles of persuasion involves giving something first to create an obligation to reciprocate?
options: Social proof
Authority
Reciprocity
Scarcity
questions.explanation: Cialdini's reciprocity principle states that people feel obligated to return favors; giving a free sample or gift triggers a felt obligation to reciprocate.

questions.question: In Fisher and Ury's principled negotiation, 'inventing options for mutual gain' means:
options: Finding the midpoint between two competing demands
Generating creative solutions that satisfy both parties' underlying interests
Letting one party get everything they want
Asking a judge to decide the outcome
questions.explanation: Inventing options for mutual gain involves brainstorming creative solutions that address both parties' real interests rather than splitting the difference on stated positions.

questions.question: Which conflict management style involves both parties giving up something to reach a middle-ground solution?
options: Competing
Collaborating
Compromising
Accommodating
questions.explanation: Compromising involves each party making concessions to reach a mutually acceptable middle-ground solution, unlike collaborating which seeks a fully satisfying win-win.

questions.question: The NCA Credo for Ethical Communication asserts that ethical communicators:
options: Prioritize winning arguments over truth
Advocate for free expression while respecting others' dignity
Keep all information confidential unless legally required to disclose it
Adapt messages to maximize persuasive impact regardless of accuracy
questions.explanation: The NCA Credo holds that ethical communicators champion free speech while treating others with dignity, sharing accurate information, and acknowledging diverse viewpoints.

questions.question: Intercultural communication competence requires which of the following?
options: Eliminating your cultural identity when communicating across cultures
Knowledge, motivation, and skill to communicate effectively across cultural differences
Speaking the native language of every culture you encounter
Adopting the norms of the dominant culture in any interaction
questions.explanation: Intercultural communication competence involves the knowledge of cultural differences, the motivation to engage across cultures, and the skill to adapt communication appropriately.

questions.question: Parasocial relationships are best described as:
options: Online friendships formed through social media interaction
One-sided emotional bonds audiences form with media figures
Professional relationships conducted via digital platforms
Peer relationships formed in group communication contexts
questions.explanation: Parasocial relationships are the one-sided emotional connections viewers develop with celebrities, YouTubers, or fictional characters who do not know the audience member exists.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the Central Route in the Elaboration Likelihood Model?
options: Persuasion that relies on superficial cues like the speaker's attractiveness
Persuasion achieved through careful consideration of the quality of arguments
Persuasion through repeated exposure to a message without conscious processing
Persuasion achieved by creating fear or anxiety in the audience
questions.explanation: The Central Route involves high elaboration — the audience carefully processes and evaluates the quality of the arguments presented, leading to lasting attitude change.

questions.question: Organizational culture is MOST directly communicated through:
options: Annual financial reports
Stories, rituals, symbols, and informal norms within the workplace
The official organizational chart and job descriptions
Government regulations governing the industry
questions.explanation: Organizational culture is primarily communicated and maintained through informal channels: stories, rituals, symbols, and the unwritten norms that shape daily behavior.

questions.question: The 'uses and gratifications' framework views media audiences as:
options: Passive recipients of media messages with no choice in consumption
Active selectors of media based on personal needs and goals
Victims of media manipulation and propaganda
Homogeneous groups who respond identically to media content
questions.explanation: Uses and Gratifications Theory portrays audiences as active agents who choose media based on specific needs (information, entertainment, social connection, identity).

questions.question: Which stage of Tuckman's model involves the group disbanding after achieving its goal?
options: Performing
Norming
Adjourning
Reforming
questions.explanation: Tuckman later added the Adjourning (or Mourning) stage to describe the dissolution of the group after completing its task or purpose.

questions.question: The digital divide is primarily a concern because it:
options: Creates too many choices for consumers
Creates unequal access to information, education, and economic opportunity
Slows the pace of technological innovation
Encourages overuse of digital devices among young people
questions.explanation: The digital divide creates significant inequalities as those without internet or device access are excluded from educational, economic, and social opportunities that are increasingly digital.

questions.question: In communication ethics, the principle of non-maleficence means communicators should:
options: Always tell the complete truth even if harmful
Avoid causing unnecessary harm through their communication
Prioritize the interests of the majority over the minority
Share information only when legally required
questions.explanation: Non-maleficence (do no harm) in communication ethics requires that communicators avoid messages or practices that unnecessarily harm individuals, communities, or society.

questions.question: Framing in mass media communication refers to:
options: The physical layout and design of a news broadcast
How media present information in ways that influence audience interpretation
The legal boundaries of acceptable media content
The selection of images used to illustrate a news story
questions.explanation: Framing is the way media package and present information — emphasizing certain aspects while downplaying others — thereby shaping how audiences interpret events.
